title: Das Lumpengesindel
source: Brüder Grimm, Kinder- und Hausmärchen, Band 1, 7. Auflage (Ausgabe letzter Hand), Dieterich, Göttingen 1857, KHM 10. Text from Wikisource (twice proofread against the original). 1857 spelling preserved exactly.
about: KHM 10, one of the Grimms’ comic rogue-tales: a cock and a hen ride out for nuts in a nutshell wagon, browbeat a scolding duck into pulling it, take on a pin and a sewing-needle as passengers, and then cheat and booby-trap the innkeeper who shelters them — leaving eggshells on his hearth, the needle in his cushion, the pin in his towel — before flitting off over the heath. It is a cascade of petty comeuppances told in a dry, mock-genteel voice, the birds forever standing on their dignity. Spelling follows the 1857 edition, so you will meet older forms such as giengen, mußte, daß, thu/that, übermüthig, rothen, Thor and Wirth.

sentences.t: Hähnchen sprach zum Hühnchen „jetzt ist die Zeit wo die Nüsse reif werden, da wollen wir zusammen auf den Berg gehen und uns einmal recht satt essen, ehe sie das Eichhorn alle wegholt.“
sentences.l: Little-cock spoke to Little-hen, “now is the time when the nuts grow ripe; there we will go together up the mountain and eat ourselves right full for once, before the squirrel carries them all away.”
sentences.i: The Cock said to the Hen, “now is the time when the nuts are ripening, so let us go up the hill together and eat our fill for once, before the squirrel carries them all off.”
sentences.n: Hähnchen and Hühnchen are diminutives of Hahn (cock) and Huhn (hen) — here almost like proper names, ‘Little-cock’ and ‘Little-hen.’ The pair speak in a mock-genteel, companionable style throughout. “satt essen” = to eat one’s fill; “das Eichhorn” is the older word for squirrel (modern Eichhörnchen). “wegholt” (wegholen, to carry off) is separable, held together here in the ehe-clause.

sentences.t: „Ja,“ antwortete das Hühnchen, „komm, wir wollen uns eine Lust miteinander machen.“
sentences.l: “Yes,” answered Little-hen, “come, we will make ourselves a pleasure with one another.”
sentences.i: “Yes,” answered the Hen, “come, let us have a merry time together.”
sentences.n: “sich eine Lust machen” = to make merry, treat oneself to a pleasure. miteinander = with one another, together — part of the pair’s cozy, well-bred tone.

sentences.t: Da giengen sie zusammen fort auf den Berg, und weil es ein heller Tag war, blieben sie bis zum Abend.
sentences.l: Then they went away together up the mountain, and because it was a bright day, they stayed until evening.
sentences.i: So off they went together up the hill, and because it was a fine bright day, they stayed until evening.
sentences.n: “giengen … fort” is the 1857 spelling of gingen (fortgehen, to go off, separable). The old -ie- spelling gieng/giengen recurs throughout this edition.

sentences.t: Nun weiß ich nicht ob sie sich so dick gegessen hatten, oder ob sie übermüthig geworden waren, kurz, sie wollten nicht zu Fuß nach Haus gehen, und das Hähnchen mußte einen kleinen Wagen von Nußschalen bauen.
sentences.l: Now I do not know whether they had eaten themselves so fat, or whether they had grown high-spirited; in short, they would not go home on foot, and Little-cock had to build a little wagon out of nutshells.
sentences.i: Now I cannot say whether they had stuffed themselves so full, or whether they had grown giddy with pride; in short, they would not walk home on foot, and the Cock had to build a little wagon out of nutshells.
sentences.n: The narrator breaks in with a shrug — “Nun weiß ich nicht … kurz” (‘I don’t know … in short’), a mock-solemn aside typical of the tale’s comic voice. “übermüthig” is the 1857 spelling of übermütig (high-spirited, cocky), with the old -th-. “mußte” keeps the 1857 ß (modern musste); “Nußschalen” = nutshells (Nuß + Schalen).

sentences.t: Als er fertig war, setzte sich Hühnchen hinein und sagte zum Hähnchen „du kannst dich nur immer vorspannen.“
sentences.l: When it was ready, Little-hen set herself inside and said to Little-cock, “you can just go right on and harness yourself in front.”
sentences.i: When it was finished, the Hen climbed in and said to the Cock, “you may as well hitch yourself up in front.”
sentences.n: “er” refers back to der Wagen (the wagon, masculine), hence ‘it.’ “vorspannen” (to harness in front, to pull the cart) is separable. “nur immer” here is a breezy, slightly imperious ‘just go right ahead’ — the Hen airily assigning the Cock the donkey-work.

sentences.t: „Du kommst mir recht,“ sagte das Hähnchen, „lieber geh ich zu Fuß nach Haus, als daß ich mich vorspannen lasse: nein, so haben wir nicht gewettet.
sentences.l: “You come to me just right,” said Little-cock, “sooner do I go home on foot than that I let myself be harnessed in front: no, that is not how we wagered.
sentences.i: “You’ve got a nerve,” said the Cock; “I’d sooner walk home on foot than let myself be hitched up: no, that was not our bargain.
sentences.n: “du kommst mir recht” is an ironic idiom — literally ‘you come to me just right,’ i.e. ‘the cheek of you! a fine idea, that.’ “so haben wir nicht gewettet” (‘that’s not what we bet’) is a set phrase for ‘that was not the deal.’ “vorspannen lasse” — lasse (1st sg. of lassen) with passive sense, ‘let myself be harnessed.’ The Cock stands mock-punctiliously on his dignity.

sentences.t: Kutscher will ich wohl sein und auf dem Bock sitzen, aber selbst ziehen, das thu ich nicht.“
sentences.l: Coachman I will indeed be, and sit on the box, but pull myself — that I do not do.”
sentences.i: Coachman I will gladly be, and sit up on the driver’s box, but pull it myself — that I will not do.”
sentences.n: der Bock is the coachman’s seat, the driver’s box. “thu” is the 1857 spelling of tu (thun = modern tun, to do). Fronting “Kutscher” and “aber selbst ziehen, das …” gives his refusal a pompous, self-important ring.

sentences.t: Wie sie so stritten, schnatterte eine Ente daher „ihr Diebsvolk, wer hat euch geheißen in meinen Nußberg gehen? wartet, das soll euch schlecht bekommen!“ gieng also mit aufgesperrtem Schnabel auf das Hähnchen los.
sentences.l: As they were quarreling so, a duck came waddling up quacking, “you thieving rabble, who bade you go into my nut-hill? just wait, that shall turn out badly for you!” and so went at Little-cock with gaping beak.
sentences.i: As they were squabbling like this, a duck came gabbling up: “you pack of thieves, who told you to go into my nut-hill? just you wait — you’ll pay for this!” and with that she flew at the Cock with her beak wide open.
sentences.n: “stritten” (streiten, to quarrel) is preterite plural. “schnatterte … daher” = came gabbling/waddling up, the sound of a duck. “Diebsvolk” = thieving rabble (Dieb + Volk). “wer hat euch geheißen” — heißen here = to bid, command (‘who told you to’). “das soll euch schlecht bekommen” is the same idiom met in Rapunzel: ‘that shall turn out ill for you.’ “gieng … los” (losgehen auf, to go at, attack) is separable; “aufgesperrtem” (aufsperren, to open wide) describes the gaping beak.

sentences.t: Aber Hähnchen war auch nicht faul und stieg der Ente tüchtig zu Leib, endlich hackte es mit seinen Sporn so gewaltig auf sie los, daß sie um Gnade bat und sich gern zur Strafe vor den Wagen spannen ließ.
sentences.l: But Little-cock was not lazy either and went at the duck vigorously, body to body; at last it pecked at her so mightily with its spur that she begged for mercy and gladly let herself be harnessed before the wagon as a punishment.
sentences.i: But the Cock was not idle either, and set upon the duck in good earnest; at last he hacked at her so fiercely with his spur that she begged for mercy and gladly let herself be hitched to the wagon as a penalty.
sentences.n: “nicht faul sein” = to be no slouch, waste no time. “jemandem zu Leib(e) steigen/gehen” = to set upon, go for someone. tüchtig here is adverbial, ‘soundly, in good earnest.’ der Sporn = the cock’s spur. “spannen ließ” — she let herself be harnessed; the punishment neatly settles the earlier quarrel over who must pull.

sentences.t: Hähnchen setzte sich nun auf den Bock und war Kutscher, und darauf gieng es fort in einem Jagen, „Ente, lauf zu was du kannst!“
sentences.l: Little-cock now set himself on the box and was coachman, and thereupon off it went at a full chase, “Duck, run for all you can!”
sentences.i: The Cock now took his seat on the box and was coachman, and off they tore at a gallop: “Duck, run as fast as you can!”
sentences.n: der Bock and Kutscher pay off the Cock’s boast in the earlier scene — he gets his coachman’s seat after all. “in einem Jagen” = at a tearing pace, in one headlong rush. “lauf zu was du kannst” = run for all you’re worth.

sentences.t: Als sie ein Stück Weges gefahren waren, begegneten sie zwei Fußgängern, einer Stecknadel und einer Nähnadel.
sentences.l: When they had driven a stretch of way, they met two pedestrians, a pin and a sewing-needle.
sentences.i: When they had driven a little way, they met two travellers on foot, a pin and a sewing-needle.
sentences.n: “ein Stück Weges” = a stretch of the road (Weges is genitive). begegnen (to meet) takes the dative: “begegneten … zwei Fußgängern.” The pin (Stecknadel) and sewing-needle (Nähnadel) join the growing comic company.

sentences.t: Sie riefen „halt! halt!“ und sagten es würde gleich stichdunkel werden, da könnten sie keinen Schritt weiter, auch wäre es so schmutzig auf der Straße, ob sie nicht ein wenig einsitzen könnten: sie wären auf der Schneiderherberge vor dem Thor gewesen und hätten sich beim Bier verspätet.
sentences.l: They called, “stop! stop!” and said it would soon grow pitch-dark, then they could go no step further, and it was also so dirty on the road; whether they might not sit in a little: they had been at the tailors’ lodging-house before the gate and had been made late over the beer.
sentences.i: They called out, “stop! stop!” and said it would soon be pitch-dark, so they could go no further; besides, the road was so muddy — might they not ride along a little? They had been at the tailors’ inn outside the gate and had lingered too long over their beer.
sentences.n: The needles’ excuses run in reported-speech subjunctive throughout: “würde … könnten … wäre … könnten … wären … gewesen … hätten … verspätet.” German marks reported claims with this Konjunktiv, a pattern seen constantly in Grimm. “stichdunkel” = pitch-dark. “einsitzen” = to get a seat (in the vehicle). “Thor” is the 1857 spelling of Tor (gate), with the old -th-. “sich beim Bier verspäten” = to be made late by lingering over the beer.

sentences.t: Hähnchen, da es magere Leute waren, die nicht viel Platz einnahmen, ließ sie beide einsteigen, doch mußten sie versprechen ihm und seinem Hühnchen nicht auf die Füße zu treten.
sentences.l: Little-cock, since they were lean folk who did not take up much room, let them both get in, but they had to promise not to tread on the feet of it and its Little-hen.
sentences.i: The Cock, since they were thin folk who took up little room, let them both climb in, but they had to promise not to step on his feet or his Hen’s.
sentences.n: “magere Leute” — a sly joke: a pin and a needle are indeed ‘lean folk.’ “Platz einnehmen” = to take up room (einnehmen, separable). “einsteigen” = to climb in, board. The promise not to tread on their feet quietly sets up the mischief to come.

sentences.t: Spät Abends kamen sie zu einem Wirthshaus, und weil sie die Nacht nicht weiter fahren wollten, die Ente auch nicht gut zu Fuß war und von einer Seite auf die andere fiel, so kehrten sie ein.
sentences.l: Late in the evening they came to an inn, and because they did not want to drive further through the night, and the duck was also not good on foot and fell from one side to the other, they put up there.
sentences.i: Late in the evening they came to an inn, and because they did not want to travel on through the night — and the duck too was unsteady on her feet and kept toppling from side to side — they turned in for the night.
sentences.n: “Wirthshaus” (1857 for Wirtshaus, inn) carries the old -th-. “einkehren” = to put up, stop for the night (separable: “kehrten … ein”). The waddling duck ‘falling from one side to the other’ is the comic reason they stop.

sentences.t: Der Wirth machte anfangs viel Einwendungen, sein Haus wäre schon voll, gedachte auch wohl es möchte keine vornehme Herrschaft sein, endlich aber, da sie süße Reden führten, er sollte das Ei haben, welches das Hühnchen unterwegs gelegt hatte, auch die Ente behalten, die alle Tage eins legte, so sagte er endlich sie möchten die Nacht über bleiben.
sentences.l: The innkeeper at first made many objections — his house was already full — and thought too, likely enough, that they might be no fine gentry; but at last, since they made sweet speeches — he should have the egg that Little-hen had laid on the way, and keep the duck too, which laid one every day — he said at last they might stay the night over.
sentences.i: The innkeeper at first raised many objections — his house was full already — and thought, too, that they might well be no persons of quality; but at last, since they spoke him fair — he was to have the egg the Hen had laid on the way, and to keep the duck as well, which laid one every day — he said at last that they might stay the night.
sentences.n: “der Wirth” (1857 for Wirt, innkeeper). “gedachte” = thought, supposed (preterite of gedenken). “vornehme Herrschaft” = fine gentry, persons of rank — the innkeeper’s snobbery is part of the joke. The bribe (the egg, and the egg-laying duck) is reported in subjunctive: “wäre … möchte … sollte … möchten.” “süße Reden führen” = to talk sweetly, coax.

sentences.t: Nun ließen sie wieder frisch auftragen und lebten in Saus und Braus.
sentences.l: Now they had fresh fare served up again and lived in rush and roar.
sentences.i: Now they had a fresh spread brought out again and lived in high revelry.
sentences.n: “auftragen” = to serve up (food), separable. “in Saus und Braus leben” is a set phrase (Saus and Braus survive only in this idiom) = to live it up, live riotously — the freeloading guests taking their ease.

sentences.t: Früh Morgens, als es dämmerte und noch alles schlief, weckte Hähnchen das Hühnchen, holte das Ei, pickte es auf, und sie verzehrten es zusammen; die Schalen aber warfen sie auf den Feuerherd.
sentences.l: Early in the morning, as day was dawning and everything still slept, Little-cock woke Little-hen, fetched the egg, pecked it open, and they consumed it together; the shells, however, they threw onto the hearth.
sentences.i: Early in the morning, as day was breaking and everything still slept, the Cock woke the Hen, fetched the egg, pecked it open, and they ate it up together; but the shells they threw onto the hearth.
sentences.n: “es dämmerte” = day was breaking (dämmern, impersonal). “pickte … auf” (aufpicken, to peck open) is separable. “verzehren” = to consume, eat up. Tossing the shells onto the hearth is the first of the tricks played on their host — the eggshells will fly into his eyes later.

sentences.t: Dann giengen sie zu der Nähnadel, die noch schlief, packten sie beim Kopf, und steckten sie in das Sesselkissen des Wirths, die Stecknadel aber in sein Handtuch, endlich flogen sie, mir nichts dir nichts, über die Heide davon.
sentences.l: Then they went to the sewing-needle, which still slept, seized it by the head, and stuck it into the innkeeper’s chair-cushion, but the pin into his towel; at last they flew, with no more ado, off over the heath.
sentences.i: Then they went to the sewing-needle, which was still asleep, seized it by the head, and stuck it into the innkeeper’s chair-cushion, and the pin into his towel; at last they flew off over the heath, without so much as a by-your-leave.
sentences.n: “packten … beim Kopf” = seized by the head (packen). das Sesselkissen = the chair-cushion; das Handtuch = the towel — the two traps laid for the host. “mir nichts dir nichts” is an idiom, ‘without more ado, just like that.’ die Heide = the heath.

sentences.t: Die Ente, die gern unter freiem Himmel schlief, und im Hof geblieben war, hörte sie fortschnurren, machte sich munter, und fand einen Bach, auf dem sie hinab schwamm; und das gieng geschwinder als vor dem Wagen.
sentences.l: The duck, which liked to sleep under the open sky and had stayed in the yard, heard them whir off, roused herself, and found a brook, on which she swam down; and that went quicker than before the wagon.
sentences.i: The duck, who liked to sleep in the open air and had stayed in the yard, heard them whirring off, roused herself, and found a brook on which she swam away downstream; and that went faster than pulling the wagon.
sentences.n: “unter freiem Himmel” = in the open air. “fortschnurren” = to whir/hum away (separable) — the sound of the pair’s flight. “sich munter machen” = to rouse oneself, get up and doing. geschwinder = quicker (comparative of geschwind). The duck slips her harness at last.

sentences.t: Ein paar Stunden später machte sich erst der Wirth aus den Federn, wusch sich und wollte sich am Handtuch abtrocknen, da fuhr ihm die Stecknadel über das Gesicht und machte ihm einen rothen Strich von einem Ohr zum andern: dann gieng er in die Küche, und wollte sich eine Pfeife anstecken, wie er aber an den Herd kam, sprangen ihm die Eierschalen in die Augen.
sentences.l: A couple of hours later the innkeeper first got out of the feathers, washed himself and wanted to dry himself on the towel; then the pin ran across his face for him and made him a red streak from one ear to the other; then he went into the kitchen and wanted to light himself a pipe, but as he came to the hearth, the eggshells sprang into his eyes.
sentences.i: A couple of hours later the innkeeper finally rolled out of bed, washed, and went to dry himself on the towel — whereupon the pin ran across his face and drew him a red streak from ear to ear; then he went into the kitchen and meant to light a pipe, but as he came to the hearth, the eggshells flew into his eyes.
sentences.n: “sich aus den Federn machen” = to get out of bed (‘out of the feathers’). “abtrocknen” (to dry off) is separable and here reflexive. “anstecken” = to light (a pipe), separable. “rothen” is the 1857 spelling of roten (rot, red), with the old -th-. Note the dative of the sufferer throughout: “fuhr ihm … über das Gesicht,” “machte ihm,” “sprangen ihm … in die Augen” — the mishaps all befall him.

sentences.t: „Heute Morgen will mir Alles an meinen Kopf,“ sagte er, und ließ sich verdrießlich auf seinen Großvaterstuhl nieder; aber geschwind fuhr er wieder in die Höhe, und schrie „auweh!“ denn die Nähnadel hatte ihn noch schlimmer und nicht in den Kopf gestochen.
sentences.l: “This morning everything is out for my head,” said he, and lowered himself peevishly onto his grandfather chair; but quickly he shot up again into the height, and cried “ouch!” for the sewing-needle had stuck him still worse, and not in the head.
sentences.i: “This morning everything is after my head,” said he, and sat himself down crossly in his big armchair; but up he shot again in a flash and cried, “Ow!” — for the sewing-needle had jabbed him still worse, and not on the head.
sentences.n: “will mir Alles an meinen Kopf” = ‘everything wants at my head,’ i.e. everything is out to get me (with the comic dative mir). “sich niederlassen” = to sit oneself down (separable: “ließ sich … nieder”). der Großvaterstuhl = a big grandfather armchair. “in die Höhe fahren” = to start up, shoot up. auweh = ouch! The prim ‘and not in the head’ is a decorous way of saying he was jabbed in the backside.

sentences.t: Nun war er vollends böse und hatte Verdacht auf die Gäste, die so spät gestern Abend gekommen waren; und wie er gieng und sich nach ihnen umsah, waren sie fort.
sentences.l: Now he was thoroughly angry and had suspicion of the guests who had come so late yesterday evening; and as he went and looked around for them, they were gone.
sentences.i: Now he was thoroughly angry, and suspected the guests who had come so late the evening before; and when he went and looked around for them, they were gone.
sentences.n: vollends = utterly, completely. “Verdacht auf jemanden haben” = to suspect someone. “sich umsehen nach” = to look around for (separable, reflexive: “sah … um” → here umsah in the wie-clause).

sentences.t: Da that er einen Schwur, kein Lumpengesindel mehr in sein Haus zu nehmen, das viel verzehrt, nichts bezahlt, und zum Dank noch obendrein Schabernack treibt.
sentences.l: Then he made an oath to take no more pack of ragamuffins into his house — such as consumes much, pays nothing, and by way of thanks plays pranks into the bargain.
sentences.i: Then he swore an oath to take no more riffraff into his house — the sort that eats a great deal, pays nothing, and, by way of thanks, plays tricks into the bargain.
sentences.n: “that” is the 1857 spelling of tat (thun = tun); “einen Schwur thun” = to swear an oath. das Lumpengesindel (the tale’s title-word) = a pack of ragamuffins, worthless riffraff. The closing verbs are present-tense general truths: verzehrt (consumes), bezahlt (pays), treibt (carries on) — “Schabernack treiben” = to get up to mischief. “zum Dank … obendrein” = ‘by way of thanks, on top of it all.’
